ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general peanut food product class; no specific roasted-salted peanut subclass exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code refers to peanut as a substance; no specific roasted-salted preparation code available. Substance-level anchor used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of roasted salted peanuts is approximately 30g (one small handful), providing around 180 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Roasted peanut, salted — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for roasted peanut, salted (cr_id F09030008). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and additional micronutrients supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 16087 (Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted, with salt) where HPB does not report those fields.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 14 for peanuts (roasted, salted) is directly reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per 30g serving calculated as: (14 × 15.26 × 0.30) / 100 = 0.64.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 320 mg/100g (salted variety) offset by high potassium (~705 mg/100g) and dietary fibre (9.4 g/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Limit portion to 30g per serving to keep sodium intake to approximately 96 mg; individuals with hypertension should prefer unsalted peanuts to eliminate added sodium while retaining the DASH-diet-compatible potassium and fibre benefits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (GI 14), high dietary fibre (9.4 g/100g), and high protein (28 g/100g) producing minimal postprandial glucose excursion
type2_diabetes.guidance: A 30g portion of roasted peanuts (GL ≈ 0.64) is appropriate as a between-meal snack for people with Type 2 Diabetes; choose unsalted varieties and avoid honey-roasted or chocolate-coated products to prevent unnecessary sugar and sodium load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Dominant unsaturated fat profile (MUFA 24.4 g + PUFA 15.7 g per 100g), zero trans fat, high fibre, and zero cholesterol support cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Peanuts consumed in a 30g daily portion are associated with reduced LDL cholesterol per AHA dietary guidelines; however, individuals with hypertension-related CVD risk should select unsalted varieties to avoid the 320 mg/100g sodium in salted products.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Dominant unsaturated fat (MUFA + PUFA) and polyphenols are anti-inflammatory, but saturated fat at 7.3 g/100g may contribute to pro-inflammatory cytokine activity at high intake
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Peanuts may be consumed in moderation (30g serving) by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis given their favourable unsaturated fat profile; choose unsalted varieties and confirm absence of peanut allergy before consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roasted salted peanuts are whole peanuts (Arachis hypogaea) that have been dry-roasted at high heat and seasoned with salt. They are a widely consumed snack food across Singapore, Southeast Asia, and globally. The roasting process produces a firm, crunchy texture and a deep, nutty flavour with a savoury salt finish. Nutritionally, roasted salted peanuts are energy-dense at 599 kcal per 100g, with an exceptionally high protein content of 28g and a dominant unsaturated fat profile (MUFA 24.4g, PUFA 15.7g per 100g), making them one of the most nutrient-dense plant-based snack options available. Dietary fibre is very high at 9.4g/100g, supporting gut health and satiety. The glycemic index is very low at 14, making peanuts one of the most diabetes-friendly snack foods per ADA and University of Sydney GI Database evidence. Sodium is elevated at 320mg/100g due to added salt, which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension; unsalted varieties are preferred for those populations. Peanuts are a significant source of niacin (13.5mg), folate (145mcg), vitamin E (6.93mg), magnesium (176mg), and phosphorus (358mg). They are naturally cholesterol-free and contain zero trans fat. Peanuts are a common allergen and must be avoided by individuals with peanut allergy. They are suitable for vegetarian, vegan, and gluten-free diets.
common_user_queries: Are roasted peanuts good for diabetics?
How much sodium is in salted peanuts?
Can I eat peanuts if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of peanuts?
Are peanuts good for heart health?
How many calories in a handful of peanuts?
What nutrients are in roasted peanuts?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F09030008, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat, fatty acid breakdown, and micronutrients supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 16087 (dry-roasted peanuts with salt), which is an exact preparation match. GI value of 14 is directly reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature.
notes: Saturated fat (7.3g), monounsaturated fat (24.43g), polyunsaturated fat (15.69g), trans_fat_g (0.0g), potassium (705mg), cholesterol (0mg), vitamins B1/B2/B3/B6/B12/C/A/D/E, folate, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water content sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 16087 (Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted, with salt) to supplement HPB data which does not report these fields. Trans fat confirmed as 0.0g per USDA SR Legacy. Cholesterol confirmed as 0.0mg (peanuts are plant-based). Vitamin C and vitamin A confirmed as 0.0g per USDA SR Legacy. Vitamin B12 and vitamin D confirmed as 0.0 (not present in plant foods). The rheumatoid_arthritis suitability entry in the input JSON contained an inconsistency: it set suitable=false but the reason text described a MUFA/PUFA carve-out supporting suitability; this has been resolved in health_context as concern_level=moderate with nuanced guidance. The dietary_tag 'wholegrain' in the original input is not applicable to peanuts and has been omitted. No -1 sentinel values remain undocumented; all -1 fields from the template have been populated with values from USDA SR Legacy 16087.
